sentences.text: You know-- I don't know if we were one of those people who didn't see the light in time or the so-called the writing on the wall in time.
But we lived up to the last minute, almost, like, no, there wouldn't be any danger around, and there wouldn't-- it could never happen, what happened.
[INAUDIBLE] was taken right-- in what-- what [?
I will-- ?] if we can start with 1942-- after 1942, they established the labor camp-- not the labor camps.
And so it wasn't a labor camp.
Jews were not allowed anywhere in the army, but they were part of the army.
But instead of-- instead of the regular-- the regular guns or-- or duties the army has, they were-- they were put into separate units.
They-- they worked on the-- on the road construction on whatever that was necessary.
So they were more or less the shovel brigade.
And-- but in the beginning, which was, I think, '42, '43, especially 1942, it was a very, very pleasant way of spending time in the army.
As a matter of fact, it was preferred to be a shovel brigade than-- than the army itself because there was no danger.
Nobody went anywhere.
They were-- it was in a 100-kilometer radius from the home town.
There was no unpleasantness about it.
As a matter of fact, at one stage, we-- my father was in-- was in the army for about a year and one-half.
Came out of these labor armies.
And for a summer holiday, we joined him in the village where he was stationed.
And we were living at the village.
We just happened to be-- we had relatives in the village.
He was in the camp.
But [? before ?] night, he-- he came home to sleep and then went back again next morning.
And as they marched through the village, and everybody was quite happy about it.
And that was-- that was at that-- then, again, of course, when they turned-- the tide has turned, and the times have changed.
And the Hungarians have joined-- have joined the Germans in the war.
Because up to 19-- up to 1942, up to 1942, the-- the Hungarians were neutral.
Hungary was not-- not an active member of the war.
They were-- they were neutral, on the side of the Germans.
On the 21st of June, 1942, Hungary entered the war and-- on the side of the Germans, and declared war on Russia.
So from then on, things happened very quickly.
From then on, the people were sent to the Russian front.

And the same time the army went to the Russian front, so the Jewish contingence also sent out, unfortunately, not in the-- not in the same comfortable way as the regular soldiers, but the same way as we were-- we were transported to Auschwitz in-- in cattle wagons-- in cattle cars.
And most of them didn't come back.
Because not only did they use them as digging trenches, but they also used them as-- as protecting themselves from the Russians.
Because they thought if the Russians will see that the Jewish brigade, with the yellow arm bands, is-- is present somewhere, they will think twice before they shoot.
But the Russians couldn't care less about who they shot.
They had a job to do.
They were defending themselves.
And-- and it made no difference.
So, unfortunately, from then on in was-- that is the time when we really got the message that something serious is happening.
What's happening-- the first thing was the Russian front.
That-- that was the beginning of the real world.
Yeah.
Yeah.
No, no, no.
That was-- that was-- it's in the wrong sequence.
Because the safe houses were only in the last year of the war.
The safe houses were not-- there were no safe houses until towards the-- towards the second half of 1944.
The Germans have occupied Hungary on the 19th of March, 1944.
And from then on, not in-- not in the cities-- so not in Budapest-- but all the surrounding suburbs, they set up ghettos.
And we entered the ghetto [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] first week of April, if I remember, something like this-- two, three weeks after the Germans occupied Hungary.

And occupied Hungary-- when I say occupied it, they only occupied Hungary because-- I don't know how much history has-- has recorded it, but I'm sure it has-- that-- that the government of Hungary, Horthy, Miklos, has shown reluctance to deport the Jews, and was-- was not going to carry through the orders of the Germans.
And he was-- he was-- but he was, unfortunately, almost the only one who-- who would have stood up to the Germans.
And, from then on, he was-- most of the time, he was on house arrest.
And he was only a figurehead after that.

So this is why-- this is the reason why the Germans occupied Hungary, to make sure that their people are in power-- their-- their people-- the Hungarian people are in power, and not the government, which they would have had difficulty with of achieving whatever they want to achieve.
So from then on, the-- the ghettos were set up, and-- and people-- almost, I would say, without an exception-- would-- they had to go and move into the ghetto.
And the ghetto was meant that they left their houses, with everything in it.
All we could take was-- we couldn't take [? anything ?] anyway, because it was too small, one family in one room, with four of us.
Four-- four-- four of us, my mother, my father, my sister, and myself.
And we were lucky to have a room to ourselves in the ghetto.
People moved out from there, whoever lived there before, they-- they-- I don't know where they put them.
And they built-- they put barbed wire around the ghetto, like every other ghetto probably.
And you had to have a pass to come in and out of the ghetto.

Now, again, at that time, food was starting to get short, and there were coupons issued for-- for anybody who was alive, whether he was a worker or a-- a-- hard labor worker, or office worker-- there were different type of coupons.
People who were-- who were useful, they were issued a bigger ration.
People who were older, or children-- I'm talking about everybody, not only Jews.
But Jews, particularly, were-- were deprived of-- of-- of food, which the non-Jews had the chance, or had the ability to, because we were in ghettos already at that time.
We were very lucky because, on the 19th of March, at the same time, it was a disaster and a-- and a catastrophe for the whole Hungarian Jewish world.
Our factory was manufacturing parts which was important for the German army-- as little as it was, but they-- they thought it was important enough.
And the so-called [INAUDIBLE] commando-- which was part of the German industrial commando-- has-- has taken over the factory.
So, in actual fact, no-- no Hungarian policeman-- or no Hungarians-- Hungarians had no authority over a factory.
Therefore--

Well, they-- they-- what happens is, that they put this [? stamp ?] out-- they put this-- [? stamp-- ?] they put the-- they-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they put the official papers out on the walls, and on the-- on the gates, that this was-- this was the-- under the-- under the authority of the [INAUDIBLE] commando.
And that meant that there was no-- nobody else.
They were the highest authority.
They were higher than the Hungarian authorities.
They-- they-- whatever we manufactured at that time went the normal way-- and went to its normal place-- but what-- what was important to them had priority.

So, at that time, and what it meant for us, as a family, that the men-- my two uncles, my grandfather, and my father-- were protected in such a way that they had safe transport, they had safe-- the whole family got plenty of-- of ration tickets.
In other words, they-- they really tried to make life a bit easier for us-- or for them.
And they came every day.
Two or three officers came, and they apologized for the inconvenience, and whatever, which didn't do much good.
But, you know, at least it was some sort of a hope that they-- that we-- we can escape what-- what we didn't escape, anyway.
Because at that time, we knew already that they were setting up in a place called Budakalasz, which is another suburb of-- of Budapest.
In the-- in the brick factories, they were setting up a so-called internation camp, where they interned all people.
But also we started to make-- to-- to have reports that that wasn't the last station, because it was sort of a transition camp, to-- to what we didn't know where to.
Now, at that time, our aim was to-- our only hope was to stay in-- in Hungary, and in Budapest.
And we-- we were hoping that we can stay in the ghetto, put it that way.
Well, most likely-- most likely I would say I was too young for that at that time.
But most likely, I would say, it was what I heard were the-- were the German officers who came to-- daily, or whatever.

Because-- I'm sure it was so, because when it came to a crucial time when-- when people were started to be transported into these brick factory from the ghettos, they came-- the Germans came and said, look, you can stay.
You can go back to your houses, but not the family-- only the men.
Only-- only the-- the heads of the family-- my two uncles, my father, and my grandfather-- but the rest of the family has to stay in the ghetto.
Of course, there's no-- no need to say that they said, no, we're not going to be separated from our family.
We don't want to move back into our houses.
There's no point in it.
We will-- we will go with out family, or stay with our family.
We'll move back to our houses if-- if our family moves back with us.

So I think after a very short time, it turned out that-- that the Hungarians had convinced the Germans that the people who were running the factory-- the foremen-- were-- were dispensable, so-- and-- and they didn't need them.

Because one or two of the-- of the managers, there were engineers-- we suspected, and we'll never know for sure-- has volunteered to take on the-- the task to run the factory and not to have-- you know, without-- without-- without the owners.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they-- the result was that the whole family was taken out and put into the-- put-- taken-- taken out from the ghetto.
And that was already in June.

Until June, we-- we managed to-- we were one of the last ones to arrive into the-- into the brick factory, which was on the [? siding-- ?] railway [? siding ?] where we eventually got loaded into the-- into the cattle trucks.
June, '44?
That was June, '44.
Yeah.
Yeah.
So we-- we-- we didn't stay very long there.
But, eventually, we had to, and I-- I remember the time when we were marched.
[INAUDIBLE] must have been hundreds of people.
We were among the hundreds of people who came last.
We were marched through the whole of the town from the ghetto-- from the ghetto to the-- to the brick factory, which was a long, long, long march, a very long march.
I-- I would say, easily, 10 kilometers-- more.
And that march, everybody could take one suitcase-- one suitcase with them.
And I remember my grandfather marching, or walking, with a-- with a stick, and a-- and a [? pack ?] on the stick, because he couldn't carry a-- a suitcase.
And that was one-- one of the last-- one of the last memories of him as I-- as I remember it.
And but what I also remember, the crowd which was lining the streets.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think that that was a time when I was really-- I was really convinced that this must be-- these people must be the scum of the earth.
Because people who-- who lived, who worked for us, people who-- who were-- were on-- really on every day terms, and I'm not singling out anybody.
But they were lining the streets but by the hundreds, by the thousands, they were spitting at us.
They were throwing stones at us-- old people, little children walking with their-- with their parents, on-- on arm, and toddlers.
I mean, just the feeling was indescribable.
There was-- there was never a matter of fear.
We were not afraid.
We were not afraid because we were past the stage of being afraid.
And, of course, we had the Hungarian gendarmes very happily carrying the-- performing their task.
In the ghetto, we had-- just to go back again to the ghetto, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the ghetto was used for interrogations.
And they used-- they didn't make any excuses for interrogations, nor-- nor did they try to do it in a discreet way.
Because they picked-- they picked one-- one building in the ghetto, which was in the middle of it, one house.
They kicked out the people who were living there at that time.
And when they interrogated people, those people, I think, they had a very hard time.
Well, the interrogations-- [INAUDIBLE] those interrogations, at that time, were mostly to-- to discover where people have hidden their-- their personal fortunes.
Some people did, some people didn't.
What people-- what they call fortunes, it was-- I don't know what they called fortunes, but they were after everything-- everything that-- and anything, what they could-- what they could get.
Because they knew that, by-- by that time, everybody knew that we-- we were being deported.
And people have hidden their-- their family jewelry, or whatever, or money, whatever the-- people were hiding.
And this is what-- what they were-- what they were after.
And anybody who I saw coming out from there, I-- I have very vivid memories, even 51 years ago.
[PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] Yeah.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] One of the people was my father.
[PAUSES FOR 24 SECONDS] And-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] and I heard him cry [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] while they were interrogating him.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]
The gendarmes and-- and the-- and the detectives.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah.
There was no difference.
There was no Gestapo.
They didn't need the Gestapo.
Yeah, detective police, or whatever.
It could have been-- it could have been special branch.
It could have been a special branch.
But they-- the Germans had full confidence, and rightly so, that the work was going to be carried out properly.
No question about it.
Who were the guards at the gates of the ghetto that people had to show their passes to?
No they were just-- just regular police.
Nobody-- nobody would have been crazy enough to-- to try to leave the ghetto without a pass.
I mean, picked-- would have picked him up and-- and beat him up, or shot him.
People were-- at that time, people already had the jitters.
And they were afraid, because, you know, there was no-- it wasn't anything unusual, people disappearing.
Nothing-- nothing unusual about it.
Had any reports filtered back, by this time, about what deportation was about?
Or--
No.
No.
We had no-- we had no knowledge of it.
Absolutely no knowledge, unfortunately, or fortunately.
I don't know which one, what-- what it was, but-- but it was good, or not good.
I don't know.
But we had no knowledge of-- we had never heard of Auschwitz.
Yes.
Yes.
A lot of people did.
A lot of people did.
They-- they-- they escaped through Romania, and-- and Turkey, and they got to Israel.
And, on the other hand, a lot of people who-- who were-- were trying the same route, they-- they made arrangements with certain groups of-- of guides, or groups.
And they paid-- they handed over their gold, or their money, what they had.
And they were slaughtered.
Whole-- whole families, and whole groups, were slaughtered half the way somewhere, wherever it-- wherever it was-- I don't know, in Turkey.
Or-- mostly in Turkey.
Or-- they never got to Israel.
That was the majority.
A few-- a few families, and a few people, got through.
And a lot of people tried.
I know that.
The other people went west.
And [INAUDIBLE] a lot.
We're talking about hundreds of people.
Many [? should ?] go west.

And would-- would my parents had the foresight and-- to take the opportunity when we could have all gone, in 1943, to-- to London-- at that time we could still go, because Hungary was at war with Russia, but not with England.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They didn't take the opportunity.
I mean, they were not the only ones, and German Jews didn't take the opportunity, other Jews.
It was-- it was something, which-- a fatal mistake.
But, again, it was the family-- the family had to stay together.
And there was no room for everybody, only-- only young people.
England didn't want to let in old people.
So my grandparents couldn't go.
So the-- the family didn't go.
No, not that I know of, anyway.
Yes.
You know, at-- at that stage we had personal articles with us.
As I said, we could take one suitcase each.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And what did-- what did people take?
They-- they took their-- their Shabbat candles with them.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Everybody-- to everybody something else was different, something priority.
Or-- we didn't know what-- what was-- we didn't know what was awaiting us, at that time.
When we moved into the ghetto, it was very quick, very new.
We didn't hear about anything else but the ghetto.
And we-- at that time we thought, when we moved into the ghetto that that was it until the end of the war.
Not really.
No, I-- I--
Well, probably.
One thing-- one thing I took, because it was with me right during the war, I still got it, but it was just a little [? siddur ?] which I carried through.
Yeah.
Yeah.
I was-- I was working in-- I was working in their own factory as a-- as a laborer, because anything was better than-- than not being able to get out of the ghetto.
[INAUDIBLE] tape two.
